message.content:
Adversarially VERIFY this research claim about how a top deep-research system scores on DeepResearch Bench.

CLAIM: MEASURED on task 72 (the exact task POLARIS is tuning): Tavily's report is 6,007 words / 1 H1 / 8 H2 / 28 H3 / 98 paragraphs averaging 20.2 words / 152 bullets / 0 tables. Its 'paragraphs' are 20-word fragments interleaved with bullet lists -- it is a structured brief, not prose.
DETAIL: Tavily's task-72 outline is 8 numbered H2 sections each split into 3-6 H3 subsections: 1. Introduction: AI as Core Driver of 4IR / 2. Core Theoretical Frameworks (2.1 Task-Based Models 2.2 Skill-Biased & Routine-Biased Change 2.3 Superstar Firms 2.4 Platformization 2.5 Organizational Complementarities) / 3. Mechanisms of Labour Market Restructuring (3.1-3.6) / 4. Heterogeneous Impacts (4.1 Skill Level 4.2 Occupation Type 4.3 Gender 4.4 Age 4.5 Geography) / 5. Industry-Specific Restructuring (5.1-5.6) / 6. Equity, Ethics, Policy (6.1-6.3) / 7. Areas of Consensus and Remaining Uncertainties (7.1 Broad Consensus 7.2 Uncertainties and Debates) / 8. Conclusion. Note sections 7.1/7.2 -- an explicit consensus-vs-open-questions section is the standard insight-farming move and appears in every top report. Across its 46 English reports Tavily averages 4,749 words, 13.2 H3+, 28.1 bullets/1k words, 0.2 table rows. Directly relevant to POLARIS: Tavily's readability (49.21) is its WORST dimension and its lowest rank-relative score -- it is the most bullet-heavy system I measured, and it is punished for it.
SYSTEM: Tavily
SOURCE GIVEN: https://huggingface.co/spaces/muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard/resolve/main/data/raw_data/tavily-research.jsonl
STATED CONFIDENCE: high

Go to the source. Check it actually says this. Check it is CURRENT (the benchmark's leaderboard and evaluator both
changed in 2026 — the original paper's numbers are stale; Gemini-2.5-Pro was replaced by GPT-5.5 as the RACE judge).
Default to UNSUPPORTED if you cannot confirm it from a primary source. We would rather have five verified facts
than thirty plausible ones.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

input.command:
cd /home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus && python3 -c "
import json,re,statistics
recs=[json.loads(l) for l in open('tavily.jsonl')]
r=[x for x in recs if x['id']==72][0]
a=r['article']
lines=a.split('\n')
h1=[l for l in lines if re.match(r'^# ',l)]
h2=[l for l in lines if re.match(r'^## ',l)]
h3=[l for l in lines if re.match(r'^### ',l)]
h4=[l for l in lines if re.match(r'^#### ',l)]
bul=[l for l in lines if re.match(r'^\s*([-*+]|\d+\.)\s',l)]
tbl=[l for l in lines if l.strip().startswith('|')]
print('words',len(a.split()))
print('H1',len(h1),'H2',len(h2),'H3',len(h3),'H4',len(h4))
print('bullets',len(bul),'tablelines',len(tbl))
# paragraphs: non-heading non-bullet non-empty blocks
paras=[]
for block in re.split(r'\n\s*\n', a):
    b=block.strip()
    if not b or b.startswith('#') or re.match(r'^\s*([-*+]|\d+\.)\s',b) or b.startswith('|'): continue
    paras.append(b)
print('paras',len(paras),'avg words',round(statistics.mean([len(p.split()) for p in paras]),1) if paras else 0)
print('--- H2s:')
for l in h2: print(l)
print('--- H3s:')
for l in h3: print(l)
"

cd /home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus && python3 -c "
import json,re,statistics
recs=[json.loads(l) for l in open('tavily.jsonl')]
a=[x for x in recs if x['id']==72][0]['article']
print('split()',len(a.split()))
print('regex \\\\w+',len(re.findall(r'\w+',a)))
print('regex [A-Za-z0-9]+',len(re.findall(r'[A-Za-z0-9]+',a)))
lines=[l for l in a.split('\n') if l.strip()]
pl=[l.strip() for l in lines if not l.strip().startswith('#') and not re.match(r'^\s*([-*+]|\d+\.)\s',l) and not l.strip().startswith('|')]
print('line-paras',len(pl),'avg',round(statistics.mean([len(p.split()) for p in pl]),1))
# strip markdown links/refs then count
print('body words excl bullets:', sum(len(p.split()) for p in pl))
# maybe words counted incl bullet text
"

input.command:
cd /home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus && python3 -c "
import json,re,statistics
recs=[json.loads(l) for l in open('tavily.jsonl')]
a=[x for x in recs if x['id']==72][0]['article']
lines=[l.strip() for l in a.split('\n') if l.strip()]
def wc(s): return len(re.findall(r'\w+',s))
cands={}
p1=[l for l in lines if not l.startswith('#') and not re.match(r'^([-*+]|\d+\.)\s',l) and not l.startswith('|')]
cands['line, w+']=p1
p2=[l for l in p1 if wc(l)>0]
p3=[l for l in p1 if wc(l)>=5]
cands['line>=5w']=p3
for k,v in cands.items():
    print(k,len(v),round(statistics.mean([wc(x) for x in v]),2), sum(wc(x) for x in v))
"

cd /home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus && cat leaderboard_gpt55_judge.csv && echo ==== && cat leaderboard_gemini25pro_judge.csv && echo ==== && python3 -c "
import json,re,statistics
recs=[json.loads(l) for l in open('tavily.jsonl')]
def iszh(s): return sum(1 for c in s if '一'<=c<='鿿')>5
en=[r for r in recs if not iszh(r['prompt'])]
print('en tasks',len(en))
W=[];H=[];B=[];T=[]
for r in en:
    a=r['article']; lines=a.split('\n')
    w=len(re.findall(r'\w+',a)); W.append(w)
    H.append(len([l for l in lines if re.match(r'^###+ ',l)]))
    b=len([l for l in lines if re.match(r'^\s*([-*+]|\d+\.)\s',l)]); B.append(b/ (w/1000))
    T.append(len([l for l in lines if l.strip().startswith('|')]))
print('avg words',round(statistics.mean(W),1))
print('avg H3+',round(statistics.mean(H),1))
print('avg bullets/1k',round(statistics.mean(B),1))
print('avg table rows',round(statistics.mean(T),2))
"

input.prompt: What judge model is used for RACE scoring currently? Is tavily-research on the leaderboard, and what are its scores (overall, comprehensiveness, insight, instruction-following, readability)? Are there separate leaderboards for different judge models (Gemini-2.5-Pro vs GPT-5.5)?

content.content:
Based on the provided webpage content, I cannot answer your questions about:

- The specific judge model used for RACE scoring
- Tavily-research's leaderboard status or scores
- Whether separate leaderboards exist for different judge models

The page snippet only shows the header and navigation elements of a Hugging Face Space called "DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard." It indicates the space is "Running" and displays a loading message: "Fetching metadata from the HF Docker repository..." 

To obtain this information, you would need to visit the actual leaderboard page once it fully loads, where the scoring details and model performance metrics should be displayed.

input.command: cd /tmp && for f in "data/leaderboard.json" "data/leaderboard_data.json"; do echo "== $f"; timeout 30 curl -sL -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}\n" "https://huggingface.co/spaces/muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard/resolve/main/$f"; done; echo "== tree:"; timeout 60 curl -sL "https://huggingface.co/api/spaces/muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard/tree/main?recursive=true" | python3 -c "import sys,json;d=json.load(sys.stdin);[print(x['path'],x.get('size')) for x in d]" 2>/dev/null | grep -iv raw_data/ | head -40

content.text:
All checks done against the primary source (live-fetched, byte-identical to the local corpus copy).

**What I verified**

1. **Source is real and current.** `curl` of the stated URL returns a 3,195,925-byte file, md5 `64ed2c35...`, byte-identical to `/home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus/tavily.jsonl`. 100 records, `{id, prompt, article}`. Record `id: 72` is the AI-labour-market literature review.

2. **Task-72 structure — CONFIRMED exactly.** 1 H1, 8 H2, 28 H3, 0 H4, 152 bullet lines, 0 table lines. Word count 6,007 is reproducible with `\w+` tokenization (whitespace `.split()` gives 5,339 — so the "6,007" figure is tokenizer-dependent, not wrong).

3. **Outline — CONFIRMED verbatim**, including the H2 titles and every H3 numbered subsection listed (2.1–2.5, 3.1–3.6, 4.1–4.5, 5.1–5.6, 6.1–6.3, and critically `### 7.1 Broad Consensus in the Literature` / `### 7.2 Uncertainties and Debates`). Note the 28th H3 is `### Sources`, so it's 27 content subsections + a sources heading.

4. **Paragraphs — substantively confirmed, minor definitional drift.** I measure 106 paragraph lines averaging 18.65 `\w+` words; total prose = 1,977 words. The claim's 98 × 20.2 = 1,979.6 — the same body-prose mass, split slightly differently. The characterization ("~20-word fragments interleaved with bullet lists; only ~1/3 of the report is prose") holds.

5. **Corpus aggregates — close but not exact.** My English subset is **50** tasks, not 46 (CJK-character detector on the prompt). Over those 50: avg 4,752.8 words (claim 4,749), 12.9 H3+ (claim 13.2), 28.7 bullets/1k words (claim 28.1), 0.18 table rows (claim 0.2). All within ~2%; the "46 English reports" denominator is the one number I could not reproduce.

6. **Readability 49.21 — real, but from the SUPERSEDED judge.** `data/leaderboard.csv` and `data/raw_results/tavily-research/race_result.txt` on the Space both give tavily-research: Overall 52.44 / Comp 52.84 / Insight 53.59 / IF 51.92 / **Readability 0.4921**. It is indeed its worst dimension in absolute terms *and* rank-relative (readability rank 27/45 vs overall rank 23/45). **However**, that file is the Gemini-2.5-Pro-judge leaderboard. The current GPT-5.5 leaderboard lives at `data_gpt55/leaderboard.csv` and contains only 10 systems — **tavily-research is not among them**. There is no GPT-5.5-judge score for Tavily.

**Caveat the parent agent should carry forward:** under the GPT-5.5 judge, readability is the lowest dimension for essentially *every* scored system (e.g. cellcog-max: 56.34/57.08/55.30 vs readability 51.94). So "Tavily is punished for being bullet-heavy" is supported by its rank-relative readability gap under the old judge, but "readability is the worst dimension" is a property of the scale, not of Tavily.

Relevant files: `/home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus/tavily.jsonl`, `/home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus/leaderboard_gemini25pro_judge.csv`, `/home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus/leaderboard_gpt55_judge.csv`.

input.reasoning:
Fetched the stated primary source live (huggingface.co/spaces/muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard/resolve/main/data/raw_data/tavily-research.jsonl); it downloads (3,195,925 B, md5 64ed2c3574031696edf96a367a4ebdaa) and is byte-identical to the local corpus copy, so the measurement substrate is authentic and current.

TASK 72 (id=72), measured directly from the article field: 1 H1, 8 H2, 28 H3, 0 H4, 152 bullet lines, 0 table lines -- all exactly as claimed. Word count: 6,007 reproduces under \w+ tokenization (whitespace .split() gives 5,339), so the figure is tokenizer-dependent but reproducible. Outline CONFIRMED verbatim, including all H2 titles and every listed H3 (2.1-2.5, 3.1-3.6, 4.1-4.5, 5.1-5.6, 6.1-6.3, and "### 7.1 Broad Consensus in the Literature" / "### 7.2 Uncertainties and Debates"). The 28th H3 is "### Sources", i.e. 27 content subsections + sources heading.

Paragraphs: I measure 106 paragraph lines averaging 18.65 words (\w+), total prose 1,977 words; the claim's 98 x 20.2 = 1,979.6 -- same prose mass, trivially different paragraph-splitting rule. The substantive characterization (short ~20-word prose fragments interleaved with bullet lists; only ~1/3 of the report is prose) is confirmed.

Corpus aggregates: my English subset is 50 tasks, not 46 (CJK detector on prompt). Over those 50: 4,752.8 avg words (claim 4,749), 12.9 H3+ (claim 13.2), 28.7 bullets/1k words (claim 28.1), 0.18 table rows (claim 0.2) -- all within ~2%. Only the "46" denominator did not reproduce.

Readability: data/leaderboard.csv and data/raw_results/tavily-research/race_result.txt on the Space both give tavily-research Overall 52.44 / Comp 52.84 / Insight 53.59 / IF 51.92 / Readability 0.4921. Readability is its lowest dimension AND its worst rank (27/45 vs 23/45 overall). CAVEAT: that leaderboard is the Gemini-2.5-Pro judge. The current GPT-5.5 leaderboard (data_gpt55/leaderboard.csv, fetched live) contains only 10 systems and tavily-research is NOT among them -- there is no GPT-5.5-judge readability score for Tavily.

Verdict CONFIRMED because the headline CLAIM is entirely about raw report structure, which is judge-independent and therefore immune to the 2026 evaluator change; every structural number checks out against the primary file.
input.corrected: Three corrections to carry forward. (1) The English-subset denominator is 50 tasks, not 46 (metric values still match within ~2%: 4,752.8 words, 12.9 H3+, 28.7 bullets/1k, 0.18 table rows). (2) Paragraph count is 106 averaging 18.65 words, not 98 averaging 20.2 -- same total prose mass (1,977 words), different splitting rule. (3) The 49.21 readability is from the SUPERSEDED Gemini-2.5-Pro judge (https://huggingface.co/spaces/muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard/resolve/main/data/leaderboard.csv). The current GPT-5.5 judge leaderboard (https://huggingface.co/spaces/muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard/resolve/main/data_gpt55/leaderboard.csv) has only 10 systems and does NOT include tavily-research, so no current-judge readability score for Tavily exists. Also, under GPT-5.5 readability is the lowest dimension for essentially every system (cellcog-max: 56.34/57.08/55.30 vs readability 51.94), so "readability is its worst dimension" is a property of the scale; only the rank-relative gap (readability rank 27 vs overall rank 23, old judge) is Tavily-specific evidence for the bullet-heaviness penalty.
